narrative:
As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 35, you've built a respectable academic foundation that many law schools value. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which aligns with the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 56.5% chance that you would fail the bar on your first attempt. This tipped the prediction toward failure, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice.

The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3rd, which is notably lower than the average 5.53; this stood out as the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward bar failure, suggesting your later undergraduate performance raised concerns about sustaining high achievement through law school and the exam. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 5th, slightly below the average 5.71, but this actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing, highlighting your stronger early momentum. Your family's upper-middle income also nudged the outlook toward failure, as the model associates certain income levels with varying bar success patterns compared to the broader dataset.

In contrast to typical students, your profile shows early promise that faded later, unlike many who maintain or improve decile rankings, which is why these academic trends dominated the decision over your above-average GPA or LSAT. While demographics like being a white male didn't directly sway the outcome here, they fit within common successful applicant patterns, making your performance dips the key differentiators. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year deciles, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or targeted GPA improvement initiative, and reapply after demonstrating upward trends. Gathering letters from professors who can vouch for your Year 1 strengths could also help contextualize your record for future reviews. With focused effort on those later performance areas, you can shift future predictions in your favor and pursue your legal ambitions.
